heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XXXI
card_summary: This chunk of Campanini's introductory essay continues tracing Zorzi's direct experience of the Holy Land, documenting how his 1493 pilgrimage shapes the exegetical content of De Harmonia Mundi and the related Problemata and Commento all'Elegante Poema. Zorzi draws on autoptic testimony alongside literary authorities (Aristotle, Plutarch, Razi, Avicenna, Jerome) to discuss Jerusalem's symbolic geography: its role as the centre of the world (attested by a Latin inscription at the Holy Sepulchre), the Temple Mount's hydraulic archaeology (the probatica pool / Betsaida), the valley of Acheldama and its miraculous decomposing earth, the Mount of Olives and the Valley of Jehoshaphat, and the sepulchre of the Virgin Mary. Hebrew etymologies (Betsaida from bet + seh) and the heterodox Christian presence in Jerusalem (seven confessions at the Holy Sepulchre) are noted throughout. Campanini shows these topographical observations are not merely devotional but feed directly into Zorzi's typological and cosmological exegesis.
essay:
## Overview

This section of Campanini's *Saggio introduttivo* (pp. 31–36) is devoted to the texture and consequences of Zorzi's firsthand pilgrimage experience — undertaken in 1493 at age twenty-seven — and the way that experience is woven into his mature theological writings. Campanini demonstrates that Zorzi is not a purely armchair exegete: his descriptions of Palestinian topography, monuments, and natural phenomena carry the mark of autopsy, and this empirical layer distinguishes his allegorical readings from those of contemporaries who relied on earlier pilgrim literature alone.

## The Date Palm as Symbol and Medical Source

Campanini opens the section by noting the date palm as a characteristic feature of the Palestinian landscape that Zorzi found both symbolically resonant and medically significant. Zorzi cites Razi (al-Rāzī) and Avicenna alongside Aristotle and Plutarch, and unusually supplements literary authority with personal observation:

> L'albero [della palma], poi, è glorioso ed è un evidente simbolo di vittoria, perché i suoi rami non si piegano mai, ma se anche sono sovraccarichi tendono sempre a rialzarsi contro la spinta del peso, come scrivono Aristotele e Plutarco e noi stessi abbiamo verificato per esperienza.

<cite>p. 31, quoting Zorzi on the palm's symbolic and experiential dimensions</cite>

The palm's indestructible uprightness becomes, in Zorzi's exegesis, a figure for the soul's resistance to worldly pressure — a tropological reading grounded, unusually, in empirical verification.

## Jerusalem as Centre of the World

Zorzi's claim that Jerusalem is the geometrical centre of the inhabited earth is supported not only by traditional typology but by an inscription he himself read at the Church of the Holy Sepulchre:

> Gerusalemme infatti è sita al centro della terra abitata, come risulta da una lapide di marmo posta nella chiesa del Santo Sepolcro sulla quale sono scritte in caratteri latini le seguenti parole (come ho verificato con i miei occhi): Questo è il centro del mondo.

<cite>p. 32, quoting Zorzi's autoptic testimony from the Problemata</cite>

This cosmological claim recurs in Zorzi's discussion of the Passion: Christ chose Jerusalem precisely because of its central, mediating position in the world-body, a position that makes Calvary the axial event of universal history. Campanini observes that this is "ribadito anche più oltre" in the text — the centrality of Jerusalem is not a passing observation but a structural premise of the entire cosmological project.

## The Holy Sepulchre and the Seven Nations

Zorzi describes the Holy Sepulchre precinct with notably precise detail, including the topography of the church built by Helena, the site of the discovery of the three crosses, and the garden where Mary Magdalene encountered the risen Christ. Most striking is his enumeration of the seven Christian confessions maintaining perpetual liturgical presence in the church:

> nel tempio che contiene quel sepolcro sette nazioni proclamano ininterrottamente inni di lode distinti: i latini, i greci, gli indiani che sono detti abissini, gli armeni, i georgiani, i maroniti, cioè i caldei e i giacobiti.

<cite>p. 32–33</cite>

Campanini notes that this catalogue of heterodox Christianities — Copts, Armenians, Georgians, Maronites, Jacobites — provokes in Zorzi something more than naive ecumenism: elsewhere the same text records his bewilderment that Jerusalem, whose praise the Psalmist sings, should be populated by "heretici, scismatici e musulmani." The tension is productive for his exegesis of the spiritual Jerusalem vs. the earthly one.

## The Probatica Pool: Hebrew Etymology and Typological Exegesis

One of the most technically accomplished passages in this excerpt concerns the pool of Bethesda (Jn 5), which Zorzi identifies with the *piscina probatica* — the washing tank for sheep at the Temple precinct. He provides both a Greek etymology (*probaton*, sheep) and a Hebrew analysis:

> In ebraico poi è detta Betsaida, vale a dire dimora delle pecore, da *bet*, che significa casa, e *seh*, che vuol dire agnello o pecora.

<cite>p. 34 (Hebrew lettering partially encoded as Unicode code points in the source)</cite>

The Hebrew terms *bet* (בֵּית, house) and *seh* (שֶׂה, lamb/sheep) are given in Hebrew script in Zorzi's text, a mark of his Kabbalistic formation. He then reads the pool's healing virtue as a typological prefiguration of baptism, instituted by "quella pecora o Agnello che tolse i peccati del mondo" — Christ as the Lamb. He dismisses the popular legend that the miraculous healing power derived from wood of the True Cross deposited in the pool, treating it with explicit scepticism: "non è forse altrettanto facile confutare questa credenza che dimostrarla?"

The same passage also contributes to Zorzi's topographical argument against those who posit two upper and lower pools in Jerusalem. On the basis of his own observation, he insists there is only one large *piscina boaria* (for oxen) inside the Temple and the smaller *probatica* outside — a correction of received exegetical tradition grounded in autopsy.

## Acheldama and the Miraculous Earth

Zorzi's treatment of the Field of Blood (Acts 1:19) focuses on its alleged ability to decompose bodies rapidly — a phenomenon reportedly reproducible even when the earth is transported to Rome. His explanation is characteristically synthetic: the virtue derives not from purely natural properties but from the contamination of the soil by the thirty pieces of silver used to betray Jesus.

> Perché i cadaveri sepolti nell'Acheldama si decompongono all'istante? Ciò è dovuto forse all'influsso del luogo? Ma come mai ciò non si verifica a poca distanza di là? Inoltre, perché la terra di quel campo ha lo stesso effetto anche se trasportata a Roma? Forse il fenomeno è dovuto a una certa virtù che quella terra ha assorbito per il fatto di essere stata acquistata proprio con le trenta monete d'argento con le quali Gesù era stato venduto?

<cite>p. 35, from the Problemata</cite>

This is a characteristic Zorzian argument: the "virtù" (*virtus*) of a place or substance is not merely accidental but inscribed by the sacred history enacted there — a position linking his topography to the broader Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic theory of sympathetic influences and the concreteness of divine action in matter.

## The Mount of Olives, the Valley of Jehoshaphat, and Eschatological Exegesis

Jesus's weeping over Jerusalem (Lk 19:41) is interpreted by Zorzi in light of his own vantage point from the Mount of Olives: Christ wept because he foresaw that the Jews would be judged in the very valley (Jehoshaphat / Joel 3:2) that lay between him and the city. Zorzi calls this valley the *val di Giudizio* and situates it precisely in relation to the Mount of Olives as he observed it. The *Commento all'Elegante Poema* (written in vernacular Venetian) adds texture:

> ivi sopra a parte destra di quella Valle è il monte Oliveto perché è pien de olive […] et ivi orò più volte, massime la sera del Giovedì avanti la passione et mostrassi anchora nella grotta ove egli orò alcune giozze di sangue ch'el sudò orando, de quai paiano resperse quelle pietre.

<cite>pp. 35–36, from the Commento all'Elegante Poema in Venetian vernacular</cite>

## The Sepulchre of the Virgin Mary

The chunk concludes with a particularly precise description of Mary's tomb in the Kidron Valley. Zorzi, through the fictional voice of the patriarch Isaac addressing him in the *Elegante Poema*, recalls his own 1493 visit: "come vedesti tu ne la tua florida gioventù, ciò è nel 27o anno de la tua età, et fò nel 1493." The tomb is described as a cave reached by 49 steps, with a small inner chamber (five feet by four) where the body rested between the Dormition and Assumption. Zorzi cites Jerome's sermon on the Assumption (*De Assumptione*) to authenticate the description.

## Broader Significance

Campanini's reconstruction of Zorzi's Palestinian itinerary in these pages serves a larger argument: that *De Harmonia Mundi* is not a purely speculative Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic text but one whose cosmological and typological claims are grounded in concrete, place-specific experience. The *omphalos* of Jerusalem, the hydraulics of the Temple, the properties of Acheldama's soil, the eschatological geography of the Kidron Valley — all feed into Zorzi's vision of a world in which sacred history has literally imprinted divine *virtus* on matter and space. This is the empirical underside of his harmonics.
